Puneet K. Sandhu, Esq., YOUNG, ZINN, PAZZANI & SANDHU LLP(213) 362-1860 psandhu@yzllp.com for Defendant LIAM BOOTH

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

DEFENDANT LIAM BOOTH Joinder In Defendant Limitless Specialty Services Associates' Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Judicial Proceedings

Defendant Liam Booth's joinder is granted.

The arbitration agreement provides, "You and the Company hereby agree that any dispute between You and the Company (including all of its parent(s), successor(s), predecessor(s)-in-interest, subsidiaries, related and affiliated companies and entities, and each of the foregoing companies' and entities' respective divisions, officers, directors, shareholders, partners, limited partners, joint ventures, agents, investors, employees, representatives, independent contractors, payroll companies, attorneys, insurers, licensees and assigns, past, present or future)." (Emmer Decl., Ex. 2 (emphasis added).)

Plaintiff's sole claim against Defendant Booth depends upon the boilerplate allegations in the underlying complaint that Booth is a "managing agent" of Limitless Specialty Services Associates, LLC ("LSSA"). As such, Plaintiff's allegation of agency is "sufficient to support a motion to compel arbitration even if the allegation is not treated as a judicial admission." (Barsegian v. Kessler & Kessler (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 446, 453 n. 3.)

As an alleged agent of LSSA, Booth fits into the class of intended third-party beneficiaries of the arbitration agreement. (See Cohen v. TNP 2008 Participating Notes Program, LLC (2019) 31 Cal. App. 5th 840, 856; Boucher v. All. Title Co. (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 262.) Furthermore, Plaintiff's Fair Employment and Housing Act ("FEHA") harassment cause of action arises out of her employment and is therefore subject to mandatory arbitration.

Finally, Plaintiff's citation to CCP 1281.2(c) [court can deny arbitration where there is a risk of inconsistent rulings] founders. The FAA applies to the parties' agreement, so the adduced rule of the CAA is displaced. (See Mastick v. TD Ameritrade, Inc. (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1263-1264.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com mailto:maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows::(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

DEFENDANT MONICA MOORHOUSE, ICONIQ CAPITAL, LLC, SQUARE SEVEN MANAGEMENT, LLC's Joinder In Motion To Compel Arbitration Of Plaintiffs Claims And To Stay Proceedings

Defendants Iconiq Capital, LLC, Square Seven Management, LLC, and Monica Moorhouse's joinder is granted.

The arbitration agreement provides, "You and the Company hereby agree that any dispute between You and the Company (including all of its parent(s), successor(s), predecessor(s)-in-interest, subsidiaries, related and affiliated companies and entities, and each of the foregoing companies' and entities' respective divisions, officers, directors, shareholders, partners, limited partners, joint ventures, agents, investors, employees, representatives, independent contractors, payroll companies, attorneys, insurers, licensees and assigns, past, present or future)." (Emmer Decl., Ex. 2 (emphasis added).)

Plaintiff's claims against Defendants depend upon the boilerplate allegations in the underlying complaint that Defendants are affiliated with Limitless Specialty Services Associates, LLC ("LSSA"). As such, Plaintiff's allegation of agency is "sufficient to support a motion to compel arbitration even if the allegation is not treated as a judicial admission." (Barsegian v. Kessler & Kessler (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 446, 453 n. 3.)

As alleged affiliates of LSSA, Defendants fit into the class of intended third-party beneficiaries of the arbitration agreement. (See Cohen v. TNP 2008 Participating Notes Program, LLC (2019) 31 Cal. App. 5th 840, 856; Boucher v. All. Title Co. (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 262.) Furthermore, all of Plaintiff's claims arise out of her employment and are therefore subject to mandatory arbitration.

Finally, Plaintiff's citation to CCP 1281.2(c) [court can deny arbitration where there is a risk of inconsistent rulings] founders. The FAA applies to the parties' agreement, so the adduced rule of the CAA is displaced. (See Mastick v. TD Ameritrade, Inc. (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1263-1264.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com mailto:maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)
